cards.front: What is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)?
cards.back: A connective tissue disease that is the most well-known and pathonomonic of connective tissue diseases, relatively more common than other forms.

cards.front: What is the sex ratio of systemic lupus erythematosus?
cards.back: SLE is 3:1 more common in women than men.

cards.front: What is the worldwide incidence of SLE?
cards.back: Approximately 1 per 1,000 to 1 per 10,000 people.

cards.front: What is the age of onset for SLE?
cards.back: Generally between 15 to 45 years, affecting younger adults.

cards.front: What populations have higher prevalence of SLE?
cards.back: African-American and Hispanic populations have greater prevalence compared to Asians and Caucasians.

cards.front: What are the main characteristics of autoimmune connective tissue diseases?
cards.back: Multi-system involvement, more common in women (estrogen plays a role), positive family history, characteristic autoantibodies, respond to immunosuppressive therapy, and high morbidity/mortality if undiagnosed or untreated.

cards.front: What are the rare forms of connective tissue disease?
cards.back: Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma), idiopathic inflammatory myositis, mixed connective tissue disease, overlap syndrome, and undifferentiated connective tissue disease.

cards.front: What is an overlap syndrome in connective tissue diseases?
cards.back: When two or more connective tissue diseases coexist in the same individual, such as SLE with scleroderma and rheumatoid arthritis.

cards.front: What is undifferentiated connective tissue disease (UCTD)?
cards.back: A term used for connective tissue diseases that do not fulfill any established criteria set.

cards.front: What are the constitutional symptoms of SLE?
cards.back: Severe constitutional illness involving fatigue, fever, arthralgia, and weight changes.

cards.front: What is Raynaud's phenomenon?
cards.back: Bluish discoloration of the tips of the fingers in response to stress or cold weather, seen in SLE patients.

cards.front: What is Sjögren's syndrome in SLE?
cards.back: A secondary condition causing dry eyes and dry mouth (sicca symptoms) associated with SLE.

cards.front: What are the musculoskeletal manifestations of SLE?
cards.back: Arthralgia, arthropathy (including arthritis), myalgia, myositis, and frank arthritis.

cards.front: What is the pathonomonic dermatological feature of SLE?
cards.back: A butterfly or malar rash, though only about 40% of SLE patients present with this sign.

cards.front: What dermatological manifestations are associated with SLE?
cards.back: Butterfly/malar rash, photosensitivity, discoid lupus rash, and alopecia (generally non-scarring).

cards.front: What are the renal manifestations of SLE?
cards.back: Nephrotic syndrome, nephritic syndrome, edema, urinary sediment changes, and hypertension.

cards.front: What are the neuropsychiatric manifestations of SLE?
cards.back: Headaches, seizures, psychosis, and mood changes.

cards.front: What are the pulmonary and cardiovascular manifestations of SLE?
cards.back: Dyspnea and pulmonary edema.

cards.front: What gastrointestinal manifestations occur in SLE?
cards.back: Nausea, dyspepsia, and abdominal pain.

cards.front: What hematological abnormalities are associated with SLE?
cards.back: Bruising related to low platelet counts, recurrent infections due to low white cell count, fatigue due to anemia, and thrombosis related to abnormal phospholipid proteins.

cards.front: How does SLE affect obstetric history?
cards.back: It may affect parity and cause miscarriages. Oral contraception and hormone replacement therapy may predispose patients to thrombosis.

cards.front: What role does family history play in autoimmune diseases?
cards.back: Patients with a family member with autoimmune disease are likely to have the same or a different type of autoimmune disease.

cards.front: What is the anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) test in SLE?
cards.back: An indirect immunofluorescence test using HEp-2 cells that is positive in more than 98% of SLE patients; reported as a titer (1:80, 1:160, 1:320, etc.).

cards.front: What is considered a positive ANA titer?
cards.back: A titer of 1:160 or higher is considered positive, as more than 10% of the general population can have lower titers without having SLE.

cards.front: What is the diagnostic specificity of double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) antibodies?
cards.back: Double-stranded DNA antibodies have high diagnostic specificity for SLE, correlate with disease activity especially lupus nephritis activity, and occur in 50-70% of SLE patients.

cards.front: What are anti-histone antibodies associated with?
cards.back: Anti-histone antibodies are present in patients with drug-induced lupus.

cards.front: What is anti-smooth muscle antibody associated with?
cards.back: Anti-smooth muscle antibodies have high diagnostic specificity for SLE.

cards.front: What autoantibodies are positive in mixed connective tissue disease?
cards.back: U1RNP (anti-U1 ribonucleoprotein) antibodies.

cards.front: What autoantibodies are associated with neonatal lupus?
cards.back: Ro, SSA, Na, and SSB antibodies.

cards.front: What autoantibodies are positive in Sjögren syndrome?
cards.back: SSA and SSB antibodies.

cards.front: What are ribosomal P antibodies associated with?
cards.back: Individuals with psychiatric disease or neurolupus.

cards.front: What are anti-phospholipid antibodies associated with?
cards.back: Patients with secondary anti-phospholipid syndrome associated with lupus.

cards.front: What are the requirements for SLE diagnosis using SLICC criteria?
cards.back: At least four criteria (at least one clinical and one laboratory) OR biopsy-proven lupus nephritis with positive ANA or positive anti-DNA.

cards.front: What are the clinical criteria in SLICC criteria for SLE?
cards.back: Cutaneous lesions, arthritis, serositis, renal involvement, neurological manifestations, and hematological abnormalities.

cards.front: What are the immunological criteria in SLICC criteria for SLE?
cards.back: ANA, anti-DNA, anti-Smith, anti-phospholipid antibodies, low C3/C4 complement, and positive direct Coombs test.

cards.front: What is the serum ANA titer requirement in ACR/EULAR criteria for SLE?
cards.back: A serum titer of ANA at least 1:80 on HEp-2 cells is required.

cards.front: How are points weighted in ACR/EULAR SLE classification criteria?
cards.back: Points are weighted according to the degree of severity or specificity for SLE; patients need 10 or more total points for classification.

cards.front: How many points are awarded for acute cutaneous lupus in ACR/EULAR criteria?
cards.back: Acute cutaneous lupus is awarded 6 points in the cutaneous domain.

cards.front: How many points are awarded for arthritis in ACR/EULAR criteria?
cards.back: Arthritis is awarded 6 points.

cards.front: What neurological symptoms earn points in ACR/EULAR criteria?
cards.back: Seizures, psychosis, and delirium earn points in the neurological domain.

cards.front: Which autoantibodies have the highest point values in ACR/EULAR immunological criteria?
cards.back: Double-stranded DNA and anti-Smith antibodies, each awarded 6 points in the immunological domain.

cards.front: What investigations are essential for diagnosing SLE?
cards.back: Blood investigations (ANA, anti-DNA, complement levels, full blood count), urine examination (dipstick, microscopy, protein quantification), and radiological investigations (X-rays, ultrasound, CT scan as needed).

cards.front: Why is urine dipstick important in SLE evaluation?
cards.back: Urine dipstick is important to look for active urinary sediment, which would indicate need for testing for lupus nephritis.

cards.front: What does an active urinary sediment in SLE indicate?
cards.back: It indicates need for investigation and testing for lupus nephritis.

cards.front: What full blood count findings are important in SLE?
cards.back: Hemoglobin (for anemia), white cell count differential (looking for lymphopenia and neutropenia, which are very common), and platelet count (thrombocytopenia can be severe).

cards.front: Why is liver function testing important in SLE?
cards.back: To look for autoimmune hepatitis and to establish baseline liver function before starting toxic medications (immunosuppressants).

cards.front: What does renal impairment testing involve in SLE?
cards.back: UN (urea and nitrogen) measurement, and if protein is positive on dipstick, spot urine sample or urine protein creatinine ratio to quantify protein.

cards.front: What should be done if infection is suspected in SLE?
cards.back: Send off a urine culture and sensitivity (MC&S) to exclude and treat infection.

cards.front: What type of arthritis does SLE typically cause?
cards.back: SLE generally results in non-erosive arthritis, showing no erosions on X-rays but possibly some gestational arthritic loss.

cards.front: When is renal ultrasound important in SLE?
cards.back: Renal ultrasound is important when planning a biopsy and to exclude obstructive causes of renal pathology.

cards.front: When is high-resolution CT of the chest indicated in SLE?
cards.back: When there is suspicion of interstitial lung disease.

cards.front: What investigations are needed for hemolytic anemia in SLE?
cards.back: Coombs test, LDH, blood smear examination, and iron studies.

cards.front: What role do complement levels play in SLE?
cards.back: C3 and C4 are important activity markers; low levels at times of lupus flare help diagnose a flare.

cards.front: What are the three anti-phospholipid antibody tests?
cards.back: 1) Anti-cardiolipin (ACA), 2) Lupus anticoagulant (LA), 3) Beta-2 glycoprotein (BGP1) antibodies.

cards.front: Why is thyroid function testing important in SLE patients?
cards.back: Autoimmune thyroiditis often occurs in patients with lupus.

cards.front: What additional autoimmune screening should be done in SLE?
cards.back: Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies (for rheumatoid arthritis), vasculitis screening for ANCA-associated vasculitis.

cards.front: Why should viral screening and RPR be done in SLE?
cards.back: To screen for coexisting viral infections and syphilis which may have similar serological findings.

cards.front: Why are lipid studies and HbA1c important baselines in SLE?
cards.back: Patients have a risk of accelerated atherosclerosis and earlier onset cardiovascular disease, and corticosteroids increase risk of diabetes.

cards.front: What is the overall management approach for SLE?
cards.back: Depends on disease severity and organ involvement; requires multi-disciplinary approach including physiotherapy and social work.

cards.front: What is initial SLE treatment?
cards.back: Glucocorticoids and specific immunosuppressant drugs as indicated by organ involvement.

cards.front: Which immunosuppressant should be given to almost all SLE patients?
cards.back: Hydroxychloroquine, provided there are no side effects and no predisposing factors preventing its use.

cards.front: What other immunosuppressants are used in SLE?
cards.back: Mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide, and azathioprine, depending on disease manifestations and organ involvement.

cards.front: What is the most important comorbidity in SLE?
cards.back: Infection, as differentiating a flare from infection and treating infection during a lupus flare can be challenging.

cards.front: Why are patients with SLE at increased risk of infection?
cards.back: Due to impaired immune system, disease activity, use of corticosteroids, and immunosuppressive medications.

cards.front: What preventative therapy is recommended for infections in SLE?
cards.back: Pneumococcal vaccine (five-year immunization) and influenza vaccine yearly, plus vigilance by patient and healthcare team.

cards.front: Why should TB always be considered in SLE with respiratory symptoms?
cards.back: Because most SLE patients are on corticosteroids which suppress the immune system, making them susceptible to TB reactivation.

cards.front: Why is cardiovascular disease screening important in SLE?
cards.back: To look for and treat accelerated atherosclerosis and hypertension (related to corticosteroids and nephritis) and dyslipidemia.

cards.front: What is corticosteroid-induced diabetes mellitus in SLE?
cards.back: A common complication; corticosteroids should be used as bridging therapy and reduced/stopped as soon as possible to prevent diabetes development.

cards.front: Why is osteoporosis a concern in SLE?
cards.back: It may be related to corticosteroid use.

cards.front: What is avascular necrosis in SLE?
cards.back: A complication sometimes associated with the autoimmune disease itself, but more commonly secondary to corticosteroid use and phospholipid antibodies; often requires hip replacement surgery.

cards.front: Why is cervical cancer screening important in SLE?
cards.back: There is increased risk related to cyclophosphamide use; female patients should receive HPV vaccines and have annual pap smears.

cards.front: What is the risk of lymphoma in SLE?
cards.back: There is a threefold increase in lymphoma risk in SLE; high clinical suspicion is needed if signs/symptoms suggest lymphoma.

cards.front: What is anti-phospholipid antibody syndrome (APLS)?
cards.back: A connective tissue disease that may occur secondary to SLE or as a primary condition, characterized by thrombosis, obstetric complications, thrombocytopenia, and skin manifestations like livedo reticularis.

cards.front: What are the clinical features of anti-phospholipid antibody syndrome?
cards.back: Venous thrombosis, arterial thrombosis, poor obstetric history (recurrent miscarriages), thrombocytopenia, and skin manifestations such as livedo reticularis.

cards.front: What are the three obstetric manifestations included in the clinical criteria for antiphospholipid syndrome?
cards.back: The three obstetric manifestations are fetal death at or beyond the 10th week of gestation, premature birth before the 34th week of gestation due to severe preeclampsia or placental insufficiency, and three or more consecutive miscarriages before the 10th week of gestation.

cards.front: What are the three antibodies tested for antiphospholipid syndrome and what is the timing requirement?
cards.back: The three antibodies are lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, and beta-2 glycoprotein. All positive antibody tests must be repeated 12 weeks later. Diagnosis requires two positive antibody tests plus one clinical criterion.

cards.front: What is the prevalence of antiphospholipid syndrome in SLE patients?
cards.back: Antiphospholipid syndrome occurs in about 30-50% of SLE patients.

cards.front: Can antiphospholipid syndrome be primary or secondary?
cards.back: Yes, antiphospholipid syndrome may be primary or secondary. Secondary APS occurs in association with other autoimmune diseases like SLE.

cards.front: What are the cardiovascular manifestations of antiphospholipid syndrome?
cards.back: Cardiovascular manifestations include cerebrovascular accidents, sinus thrombosis, cardiac valve disease, and myocardial infarction.

cards.front: What is the pathognomonic dermatological manifestation of antiphospholipid syndrome?
cards.back: Livedo reticularis is the pathognomonic dermatological manifestation - a net-like rash that may be present on the thighs or aspects of the arms.

cards.front: What other skin manifestations can occur in antiphospholipid syndrome?
cards.back: Patients may present with purpura, infarction, or ulceration involving the skin.

cards.front: What hematological manifestations are associated with antiphospholipid syndrome?
cards.back: Common hematological manifestations include thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, and arterial or venous thromboembolic disease.

cards.front: What neurological manifestations can occur in antiphospholipid syndrome?
cards.back: Neurological manifestations include stroke, TIAs, and cognitive dysfunction.

cards.front: What respiratory manifestations can occur in antiphospholipid syndrome?
cards.back: Patients may develop oculothrombosis, pulmonary embolus, and pulmonary hypertension.

cards.front: What is scleroderma and what is its female to male ratio?
cards.back: Scleroderma is diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis. It has a high female preponderance with a female to male ratio of 4:1.

cards.front: What are the etiological factors in scleroderma?
cards.back: Etiology may be related to certain occupations, exposure to chemicals and chemical agents, and certain drugs. However, most cases are idiopathic with possible genetic predisposition.

cards.front: What is the peak age of onset for scleroderma?
cards.back: The peak age of onset for scleroderma is 35 to 64 years of age.

cards.front: What is the incidence of scleroderma?
cards.back: Scleroderma occurs at approximately 30 persons per million population per year, with an estimated 125,000 active cases in the United States.

cards.front: How does scleroderma differ between African-American and European-American women?
cards.back: Scleroderma occurs at a younger age in African-American women compared to European-American women and is more likely to be diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis in African-Americans.

cards.front: Which population has the highest reported incidence of scleroderma?
cards.back: The Choctaw Native Americans residing in Oklahoma have the highest reported disease incidence of scleroderma.

cards.front: What are the key pathogenic processes involved in scleroderma?
cards.back: Pathogenesis involves vascular endothelial dysfunction and fibrotic process. Etiological agents or genetic predisposition lead to endothelial cell abnormalities and vascular alterations, causing chronic inflammation, fibrocyte proliferation, myofibroblast activation, vascular dysfunction, and tissue fibrosis.

cards.front: What are the key characteristics of scleroderma?
cards.back: Key characteristics include skin thickening, Raynaud phenomenon, scleroderma and most patients have a positive antinuclear antibody test (>90%).

cards.front: How is scleroderma classified?
cards.back: Scleroderma is classified into localized scleroderma (morphia, not associated with systemic manifestations) and diffuse scleroderma (associated with systemic manifestations and referred to as systemic sclerosis).

cards.front: What are the two types of systemic sclerosis?
cards.back: Systemic sclerosis is divided into diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis and limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis. There is also a small percentage of patients with systemic sclerosis without scleroderma.

cards.front: How does skin involvement differ between limited and diffuse scleroderma?
cards.back: In limited scleroderma, skin thickening is distal to elbows and knees. In diffuse, skin thickening is proximal to elbows and knees and may involve the trunk. Both may involve the face.

cards.front: What are the three phases of skin changes in scleroderma?
cards.back: The three phases are the edematous phase (early), the indurative phase (skin thickening), and the atrophic phase (contractures and loss of hair and skin appendages).

cards.front: What is sclerodactyl?
cards.back: Sclerodactyl is tightening of the skin distal to the metacarpophalangeal joints.

cards.front: What facial changes occur in scleroderma?
cards.back: Due to skin tightening over the face, patients develop an expressionless face with pursed lips and reduced oral aperture (microstomia).

cards.front: What is telangiectasia and where is it found in scleroderma?
cards.back: Telangiectasia consists of spider-like vessels that are commonly found on the lips, buccomucosa, chest, and face.

cards.front: What is calcinosis in scleroderma?
cards.back: Calcinosis refers to calcific deposits found in subcutaneous tissues in patients with scleroderma.

cards.front: What hyperpigmentation features occur in scleroderma?
cards.back: Hyper- or hyperpigmentation of the skin, salt-and-pepper rash can occur in scleroderma.

cards.front: What is the difference between primary and secondary Raynaud phenomenon?
cards.back: Primary Raynaud is not always pathological, affects 4-15% of population with female to male ratio 20:1, and is managed with conservative measures and vasodilators. Secondary Raynaud is associated with connective tissue disease, often with abnormal nailfold capillary changes and digital ulceration.

cards.front: What is the characteristic color response pattern in Raynaud phenomenon?
cards.back: The tri-phasic color response consists of white (ischemia), blue/purple (deoxygenation), and red (reperfusion).

cards.front: When does secondary Raynaud phenomenon typically occur in relation to connective tissue disease?
cards.back: Secondary Raynaud generally occurs within two years of onset of connective tissue disease.

cards.front: What is the prevalence of Raynaud phenomenon in limited versus diffuse scleroderma?
cards.back: Raynaud is present in 100% of limited scleroderma patients and in 70-80% of diffuse scleroderma patients. In SLE, 20-45% may have Raynaud.

cards.front: What is the ACR/EULAR classification criteria cutoff for definite scleroderma?
cards.back: A patient should have 9 or more points to be classified as having definite scleroderma. The sensitivity is 91% with 92% specificity.

cards.front: What antibodies are associated with diffuse scleroderma?
cards.back: Diffuse scleroderma is associated with positive SCL-70 antibodies and positive antinuclear antibody.

cards.front: What manifestations occur in the first 1-12 months of diffuse scleroderma?
cards.back: In diffuse scleroderma, Raynaud phenomenon, progressive skin thickening, tendon friction rubs, gastrointestinal involvement, arthritis, and fatigue may occur within the first 1-12 months.

cards.front: What should be screened for in systemic sclerosis to detect internal organ involvement?
cards.back: Screening should focus on pulmonary hypertension, lung fibrosis, renal involvement, cardiac disease, and gastrointestinal symptoms.

cards.front: What conservative management is used for Raynaud phenomenon?
cards.back: Management includes keeping warm using gloves or socks and stopping smoking.

cards.front: What vasodilators are used for Raynaud phenomenon?
cards.back: Calcium channel blockers such as nifedipine, diltiazem, and amlodipine are used. The dose is increased until desired effect is obtained or stopped if side effects occur.

cards.front: What other medications are used for Raynaud phenomenon?
cards.back: Anti-adrenergic agents (prazosin, doxazosin), ACE inhibitors, and ARBs (losartan, valsartan, bisartan) are used, though they are less effective than calcium channel blockers.

cards.front: How is topical nitroglycerin used in Raynaud phenomenon?
cards.back: Topical nitroglycerin ointment may be applied sparingly over affected areas for 20 minutes three times a day for painful digital ulceration. Headaches may occur as a side effect.

cards.front: What role does aspirin play in Raynaud phenomenon management?
cards.back: Half an aspirin per day may be beneficial to inhibit platelet activation and is often recommended in patients with Raynaud.

cards.front: What is the annual incidence of idiopathic inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: The annual incidence is 2-10 cases per million, making it a relatively rare disease.

cards.front: What is the bimodal age of onset for dermatomyositis and polymyositis?
cards.back: Dermatomyositis has a bimodal distribution with peaks at 5-15 years and 45-65 years. Polymyositis is rare before age 15 with mean onset between 50-60 years.

cards.front: What is the female to male ratio for inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: The female to male ratio is 2-3:1, with predominantly females affected.

cards.front: How does incidence of myositis differ by race in the United States?
cards.back: African Americans have a greater incidence of myositis compared to Caucasians.

cards.front: What is the typical clinical presentation of inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Patients present with proximal muscle weakness involving the shoulder and hip girdle, with difficulty standing, combing hair, rising from seated position, and walking upstairs. Neck flexion weakness may occur.

cards.front: What percentage of myositis patients have muscle pain?
cards.back: Muscle pain is present in about 50% of patients and may be associated with muscle edema or swelling.

cards.front: What is heliotrope rash and where does it occur in dermatomyositis?
cards.back: Heliotrope rash is a violaceous rash that involves the peri-orbital area in patients with dermatomyositis.

cards.front: What are Gottron papules?
cards.back: Gottron papules are scaly lesions found on the extensor surfaces of the joints, particularly on the PIP, MCP, and sometimes elbow joints.

cards.front: What is the V-sign in dermatomyositis?
cards.back: The V-sign is a hyperpigmented photosensitive rash in the V of the neck.

cards.front: What is the shawl sign in dermatomyositis?
cards.back: The shawl sign is a rash involving the shoulders and proximal arms.

cards.front: What is the holster sign in dermatomyositis?
cards.back: The holster sign is a rash involving the lateral aspect of the thighs.

cards.front: What are mechanic's hands in inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Mechanic's hands involve thickening, fissuring, and cracking of the skin of the finger pads, particularly on the radial side of the index finger. This is associated with anti-synthetase antibody positivity and anti-synthetase syndrome.

cards.front: What nailfold abnormalities occur in inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Peri-ungual erythema and hypertrophy with dilated capillary loops may be seen in inflammatory myositis.

cards.front: Where is subcutaneous calcification particularly seen in myositis?
cards.back: Subcutaneous calcification is particularly seen in juvenile dermatomyositis.

cards.front: What are the classification subtypes of idiopathic inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Classification includes polymyositis, dermatomyositis, inclusion body myositis, myositis associated with connective tissue diseases, and myositis associated with malignancy.

cards.front: What are the characteristic features of polymyositis?
cards.back: Polymyositis is characterized by proximal muscle weakness in limb-girdle and neck flexor muscles, elevated creatinine kinase, muscle biopsy evidence of myositis, and abnormal EMG findings.

cards.front: Why is malignancy screening important in inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Always maintain high index of suspicion for underlying malignancies, particularly in older age individuals with dermatomyositis. Malignancy screening is important.

cards.front: What extra-muscular manifestations may occur in inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Extra-muscular features include lung involvement with interstitial lung disease (associated with anti-synthetase syndrome or polymyositis overlap), and scleroderma dysmotility.

cards.front: What other causes should be excluded in proximal muscle weakness?
cards.back: Other causes to exclude include drug and toxin-induced myopathies, neuromuscular disorders, endocrine disorders, sarcoidosis, rheumatic disorders, infections, and metabolic myopathies (particularly hypokalemia).

cards.front: Why should potassium levels be checked in proximal muscle weakness?
cards.back: Hypokalemia is a common metabolic abnormality causing proximal muscle weakness that can be easily replaced and rectified.

cards.front: What variables are included in the 2017 EULAR/ACR diagnostic criteria for inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Variables include age of onset (bimodal: 18-40 and >40 years), clinical muscle variables (upper/lower extremity and neck flexor weakness), skin variables, laboratory variables (elevated CK, LDH, AST, ALT, positive antinuclear antibody), and muscle biopsy findings.

cards.front: What are the characteristic muscle biopsy findings in inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Muscle biopsy may show endomysial infiltrate, perimysial infiltrate, perifascicular atrophy, and rimmed vacuoles in inclusion body myositis.

cards.front: What is the Bohan and Peter classification criteria?
cards.back: The Bohan and Peter classification criteria is a relatively simple classification criteria that assists in making a diagnosis of inflammatory myositis.

cards.front: What are the myositis subtypes in the new classification criteria?
cards.back: New subtypes include idiopathic inflammatory myositis (now called juvenile dermatomyositis), polymyositis, inclusion body myositis, immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy, seronegative dermatomyositis, antisynthetase syndrome, overlap myositis, cancer-associated myositis, and juvenile dermatomyositis.

cards.front: What myositis-specific antibody is most commonly found in juvenile dermatomyositis?
cards.back: Anti-Mi2 antibody is most commonly found in juvenile dermatomyositis.

cards.front: What antibodies are associated with necrotizing myositis?
cards.back: Anti-signal recognition peptide (SRP) antibody is associated with necrotizing myositis.

cards.front: What antibody is associated with polymyositis and antisynthetase syndrome?
cards.back: Anti-Jo-1 antibody is associated with polymyositis and antisynthetase syndrome.

cards.front: What is the inverted pyramid approach to treating inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: The inverted pyramid consists of corticosteroids as the base, steroid-sparing agents as the next level, and treatment of refractory disease at the top. Most patients respond to corticosteroids alone, some require steroid-sparing agents, and fewer have refractory disease.

cards.front: What is mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD)?
cards.back: Mixed connective tissue disease is an autoimmune disease with its own set of criteria that combines features of multiple connective tissue diseases.

cards.front: What are the Alarcon-Segovia diagnostic criteria for MCTD?
cards.back: The criteria include serological criteria (anti-U1-RNP antibody positive in high titer) and clinical criteria (edema of hands, sinovitis, myositis, Raynaud phenomenon, acrosclerosis).

cards.front: What is the significance of anti-U1-RNP antibody in MCTD diagnosis?
cards.back: One must have anti-U1-RNP antibody positive in high titer together with the presence of clinical criteria to make the diagnosis. Presence of U1-RNP antibody alone without clinical criteria does not make the diagnosis of MCTD.

cards.front: What are the clinical features of MCTD?
cards.back: Clinical features include edema of the hands, sinovitis (joint inflammation), myositis (muscle inflammation), Raynaud phenomenon, and acrosclerosis (skin tightening of the digits).

cards.front: What is gout and what causes it?
cards.back: Gout is a form of immunoarthritis caused by the deposition of monosodium urate (MSU) crystals in joints and surrounding tissues. MSU shedding in a joint leads to recurring episodes of acute inflammatory arthritis.

cards.front: What is hyperuricemia and what does it mean?
cards.back: Hyperuricemia is a serum uric acid concentration above the saturation point (~6.8 mg/dL at body temperature). Clinical consequences include acute uric acid stones and nephropathies.

cards.front: What are the primary causes of hyperuricemia?
cards.back: Primary causes (genetic predisposition) or secondary causes - overproduction of uric acid or under-excretion of uric acid. Under-excretion is the main cause of hyperuricemia.

cards.front: What is the male-to-female ratio and age of onset for gout?
cards.back: Gout is the most common form of immunoarthritis in men. Male-to-female ratio is 2-4:1 in Caucasians and 8:1 in Asians. Peak age is 40-50 years. Very rare in males below age 30 and in women.

cards.front: Do all people with hyperuricemia develop gout?
cards.back: No. Not everyone with hyperuricemia develops gout. Many people remain asymptomatic despite elevated uric acid levels.

cards.front: Where does uric acid come from in the body?
cards.back: Uric acid comes from protein-based diet. Nucleic acids (adenine, guanine) are converted to hypoxanthine and xanthine, then to uric acid via the enzyme xanthine oxidase.

cards.front: Why do humans accumulate uric acid compared to other primates?
cards.back: In humans and higher primates, uricase (a hepatic enzyme that converts uric acid to the more soluble allantoin) is inactive due to a non-sense mutation. This prevents conversion of uric acid to the more soluble form, so uric acid accumulates.

cards.front: How is uric acid eliminated from the body?
cards.back: Approximately 60-70% of daily uric acid is excreted by the kidneys. About 10% undergoes uricolysis in the gut. The amount of uric acid in the body is the natural result of production minus elimination.

cards.front: What conditions cause overproduction of uric acid?
cards.back: High-purine foods, myeloproliferative disorders, psoriasis, and cytotoxic drugs cause overproduction of uric acid.

cards.front: What conditions and drugs cause under-excretion of uric acid?
cards.back: Alcohol, renal disease, hypertension, and drugs like diuretics, penicillamine, and thiazide diuretics cause under-excretion of uric acid.

cards.front: What is the relationship between uric acid and metabolic syndrome?
cards.back: There is an association between uric acid and metabolic syndrome (obesity, hypertension, hypertriglyceridemia, cholesterol disease, and diabetes). Reduction in uric acid clearance is associated with these conditions.

cards.front: What are the consequences of asymptomatic hyperuricemia?
cards.back: A patient with asymptomatic hyperuricemia may remain asymptomatic, develop acute gout attacks, develop tophi, chronic tophaceous gout, or uric acid neuropathy. Only a small percentage develop gout. Risk of kidney stones and kidney damage is low. Monitoring is important.

cards.front: What is the typical timing and presentation of an acute gout attack?
cards.back: Acute gout onset is sudden with acute inflammation. Maximum risk of pain within 12-24 hours. Patients present with signs of acute inflammation that usually resolve in 5-10 days of treatment.

cards.front: Which joints are involved in gout attacks?
cards.back: Gout can be monoarticular or polyarticular. About 50% of first attacks involve the first metatarsophalangeal joint. Lower limbs are clearly affected. In 8% of patients in the first attack, other joints like the elbow can be involved.

cards.front: Who typically develops polyarticular gout?
cards.back: Polyarticular gout is often seen in older patients taking diuretics and in females.

cards.front: Is uric acid level always elevated during an acute gout attack?
cards.back: No. Uric acid level may be normal during an acute gout attack. Therefore, diagnosis relies on history and examination. A normal uric acid level does not exclude gout or acute gout.

cards.front: What are the physical signs of acute gout?
cards.back: The affected joint is red, warm, and tender. Acute inflammation is evident. The ankle bones or knee to foot can also be affected. The attack is striking with pain and swelling.

cards.front: What is chronic tophaceous gout?
cards.back: Chronic tophaceous gout is widespread deposition of urate in joints, tendons, and cartilage. Tophi develop in about 10 years when hyperuricemia is undiagnosed, untreated, or inadequately managed. Can present in elderly women and may damage bones and joints.

cards.front: What do tophi look like and where can they be found?
cards.back: Tophi contain white/chalky material. They can be seen in the ears, hands, and other joints. X-rays show destruction of bone. Material can discharge resembling chalky particles.

cards.front: What is the relationship between gout and kidney stones?
cards.back: Patients may develop nephropathies, including uric acid nephrolithiasis (kidney stones). Risk is related to the level of uric acid and urinary uric acid. Factors favoring stone production include high uric acid, dehydration, reduced urine volume, and reduced pH.

cards.front: What types of kidney disease are associated with gout?
cards.back: Three types - (1) Uric acid nephrolithiasis (kidney stones), (2) Chronic uric acid nephropathy, (3) Acute uric acid nephropathy (tumor lysis syndrome) due to massive overproduction and chemotherapy.

cards.front: What investigations should be done for suspected gout?
cards.back: Should measure serum uric acid, serum creatinine, and renal function. Check for uric acid in urine. Examine synovial fluid (arthrocentesis) to confirm crystals. Screen for associated conditions and measure blood pressure.

cards.front: What is the definitive diagnostic test for gout?
cards.back: Definitive diagnosis is made by demonstration of monosodium urate crystals (needle-shaped) in synovial fluid obtained by joint aspiration. Synovial fluid appearance varies but is often clear or slightly turbid.

cards.front: What characteristics of synovial fluid suggest sepsis in gout?
cards.back: The more turbid the synovial fluid, the more likely actual sepsis is present. Most of the time the fluid is clear or slightly cloudy.

cards.front: How should asymptomatic hyperuricemia be managed?
cards.back: For asymptomatic hyperuricemia - monitor patients seriously without treatment. No specific treatment is required unless hyperuricemia correction is possible. Management focuses on patient monitoring.

cards.front: What is the first-line acute management of gout?
cards.back: First-line is NSAIDs, but must be used with caution. Avoid in active ulcers and renal insufficiency. Alternative - Colchicine with starting dose of 1 mg, then 0.5 mg after 2 hours, then 0.5 mg twice daily until resolution.

cards.front: What is the typical colchicine dosing regimen?
cards.back: Colchicine - 0.5-1 mg 2-3 times daily for 5-10 days if losing all medication, or 2.5 mg then 2-5 days for those not losing medication. Alternative - 0.5 mg 3-4 times daily then taper over 7-10 days.

cards.front: When can intramuscular corticosteroids be used in acute gout?
cards.back: Intramuscular corticosteroids can be used in acute gout as an alternative to NSAIDs and colchicine, especially for patients who cannot tolerate NSAIDs or colchicine.

cards.front: Should urate-lowering therapy be started during an acute gout attack?
cards.back: Urate-lowering therapy should not be initiated during acute attack due to chemotaxis concerns or prolonged attack. If patient is already on urate-lowering therapy, it can be continued while avoiding sudden discontinuation.

cards.front: What are the components of long-term gout management?
cards.back: Long-term management includes - (1) Patient education, (2) Diet modification, (3) Obesity and alcohol reduction, (4) Long-term prophylactic therapies (NSAIDs, colchicine for 3-6 months), (5) Urate-lowering therapy.

cards.front: How long should prophylactic therapy be continued?
cards.back: NSAIDs or colchicine for 3-6 months or until uric acid levels normalize. Low-dose colchicine can continue for 6 months or until uric acid level returns to normal.

cards.front: What are the indications for long-term urate-lowering therapy?
cards.back: Indications - (1) Serum uric acid >8-9 mg/dL with symptoms, (2) More than two attacks of gout, (3) Presence of tophi, (4) Patient willing to continue therapy long-term.

cards.front: What is allopurinol and how does it work?
cards.back: Allopurinol is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor that acts on the last pathway converting xanthine to uric acid. It prevents conversion of xanthine to uric acid to stop uric acid accumulation. Alternative - febuxostat (xanthine oxidase inhibitor).

cards.front: How do uricosuric agents work?
cards.back: Uricosuric agents increase uric acid excretion by the kidneys. They work by blocking uric acid reuptake in kidney tubules, increasing urinary excretion of uric acid.

cards.front: What are the indications for uricosuric agents?
cards.back: Indications - (1) Normal uric acid excretion, (2) Creatinine clearance >60 mL/minute, (3) No history of uric acid stones, (4) Age below 60 years, (5) Patient does not develop attacks with low-dose therapy.

cards.front: What are the indications for xanthine oxidase inhibitors like allopurinol?
cards.back: Indications - (1) Increased uric acid excretion, (2) Renal insufficiency, (3) Nephrolithiasis, (4) Uricosuric agent intolerance, (5) Prophylaxis for tumor lysis syndrome.

cards.front: What four clinical adjectives define the standard presentation of Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA)?
cards.back: Chronic, symmetrical, inflammatory, and erosive polyarthritis.

cards.front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what is the 'formula' for determining joint destruction?
cards.back: $\text{Inflammation} \times \text{Time} = \text{Joint Destruction}$.

cards.front: Which specific MHC class II gene shows the strongest association with Rheumatoid Arthritis in most populations?
cards.back: $HLA-DR4$.

cards.front: What environmental factor is most significantly associated with both increased risk and greater disease severity in Rheumatoid Arthritis?
cards.back: Smoking.

cards.front: What is the typical duration of morning stiffness in a patient with Rheumatoid Arthritis?
cards.back: More than 30 minutes.

cards.front: Which three pro-inflammatory cytokines are primarily produced by activated synovial macrophages in RA?
cards.back: $TNF$, $IL-1$, and $IL-6$.

cards.front: In Rheumatoid Arthritis, what is the effect of $RANKL$ on bone?
cards.back: It activates osteoclasts, leading to bone destruction.

cards.front: Identify the characteristic hand deformity in RA involving hyperextension of the PIP joint and flexion of the DIP joint.
cards.back: Swan neck deformity.

cards.front: Identify the characteristic hand deformity in RA involving flexion of the PIP joint and hyperextension of the DIP joint.
cards.back: Boutonniere deformity.

cards.front: Under the 2010 ACR/EULAR classification criteria, what is the minimum score required to diagnose definite Rheumatoid Arthritis?
cards.back: A score of $\ge 6$.

cards.front: What are the four clinical domains assessed in the ACR/EULAR 2010 criteria for RA?
cards.back: Joint involvement, Serology, Duration of synovitis, and Acute-phase reactants.

cards.front: In the context of RA, what does the 'Piano key sign' indicate regarding the wrist?
cards.back: Instability or subluxation of the ulnar styloid.

cards.front: Which ocular manifestation of RA involves severe thinning of the sclera that may lead to perforation?
cards.back: Scleromalacia perforans.

cards.front: What are the three components of Felty Syndrome?
cards.back: Rheumatoid arthritis, splenomegaly, and neutropenia (uropinea).

cards.front: What is the primary objective of using 'bridging therapy' with steroids in Rheumatoid Arthritis?
cards.back: To control inflammation rapidly while waiting for DMARDs to become effective.

cards.front: According to the EULAR Disease Activity Score, what score range indicates 'Remission'?
cards.back: A score $< 2.6$.

cards.front: Which class of drugs includes Revellex and Etanercept in the treatment of RA?
cards.back: Tumor necrosis factor-alpha ($TNF$-$\alpha$) blocking drugs (Biologic DMARDs).

cards.front: What is the most critical screening requirement before initiating biologic DMARD therapy?
cards.back: Screening for latent or active infections, particularly Tuberculosis (TB).

cards.front: How does the pattern of joint involvement in Spondyloarthropathy (SpA) typically differ from Rheumatoid Arthritis?
cards.back: SpA typically involves the axial skeleton and presents as asymmetrical arthritis of large lower limb joints.

cards.front: Define 'enthesitis', a prominent feature of Spondyloarthropathies.
cards.back: Inflammation at the site of insertion of a tendon, ligament, or articular capsule into bone.

cards.front: Which genetic antigen is highly associated with the Spondyloarthropathy group of diseases?
cards.back: $HLA-B27$.

cards.front: List the five main subtypes of Spondyloarthritis (SpA).
cards.back: Ankylosing spondylitis, Psoriatic arthritis, Reactive arthritis, Enteropathic arthritis, and Juvenile ankylosing spondylitis.

cards.front: What is the classic clinical triad associated with Reactive Arthritis (formerly Reiter’s Syndrome)?
cards.back: Conjunctivitis, Arthritis, and Urethritis.

cards.front: Which urogenital pathogen is a common trigger for Reactive Arthritis?
cards.back: Chlamydia trachomatis.

cards.front: What is 'Keratoderma blenorrhagicum'?
cards.back: A papulosquamous rash on the palms and soles associated with Reactive Arthritis.

cards.front: In Spondyloarthritis, what does the term 'Dactylitis' refer to?
cards.back: Diffuse swelling of a whole finger or toe ('sausage digit').

cards.front: Which specific radiographic finding in the spine is characteristic of advanced Ankylosing Spondylitis?
cards.back: Bamboo spine (due to syndesmophytes).

cards.front: What is the primary site of disease in Osteoarthritis (OA)?
cards.back: Hyaline articular cartilage.

cards.front: Define the pathology of Osteoarthritis regarding cartilage and bone.
cards.back: Focal softening and disintegration of cartilage accompanied by proliferation of new bone (osteophytes).

cards.front: List three cardinal radiographic features of Osteoarthritis.
cards.back: Joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and marginal osteophytes.

cards.front: In hand Osteoarthritis, what is the name for nodes located at the Distal Interphalangeal (DIP) joints?
cards.back: Heberden's nodes.

cards.front: In hand Osteoarthritis, what is the name for nodes located at the Proximal Interphalangeal (PIP) joints?
cards.back: Bouchard’s nodes.

cards.front: What mechanical factor increases the risk of knee OA four-fold when the $BMI$ is $> 30$?
cards.back: Obesity.

cards.front: Gout results from the deposition of which specific type of crystals?
cards.back: Monosodium urate monohydrate crystals.

cards.front: At what serum uric acid concentration threshold does the risk of crystal formation significantly increase?
cards.back: $> 0.4 \text{ mmol/L}$.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of hyperuricaemia: overproduction or underexcretion of uric acid?
cards.back: Underexcretion (accounts for 80% of cases).

cards.front: What is the peak age range for the onset of Gout in males?
cards.back: 40 to 50 years.

cards.front: Why is Gout rare in premenopausal women?
cards.back: The uricosuric effects of oestrogen help maintain lower uric acid levels.

cards.front: Which joint is involved in 50% of first-time acute gout attacks?
cards.back: The first metatarsophalangeal (1st MTP) joint (Podagra).

cards.front: Define 'Tophi' in the context of chronic Gout.
cards.back: Accumulations of urate crystals in cartilage, tendons, bursae, and bones.

cards.front: What is the gold standard investigation for a definitive diagnosis of Gout?
cards.back: Demonstration of uric acid crystals in synovial fluid or tophaceous material.

cards.front: What is the mechanism of action for Allopurinol in treating Gout?
cards.back: It is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor that reduces the production of uric acid.

cards.front: Name a uricosuric agent used for long-term management of Gout.
cards.back: Probenecid (or Lesinurad).

cards.front: What acute renal complication can occur during chemotherapy due to massive uric acid excretion?
cards.back: Acute uric acid nephropathy (Tumour Lysis Syndrome).

cards.front: Which autoantibody test is the primary screening tool for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)?
cards.back: Antinuclear factor (ANF) or Antinuclear antibody (ANA).

cards.front: What is considered a clinically significant positive titre for an ANA test?
Identify the characteristic 'triphasic color response' seen in Raynaud's Phenomenon.
cards.back: White (ischaemia), Blue (hypoxia), and Red (reperfusion).

cards.front: In Systemic Sclerosis (Scleroderma), what are the three sequential phases of skin changes?
cards.back: Oedematous phase, Indurable (thickening) phase, and Atrophic phase.

cards.front: What are the two hallmark skin rashes associated with Dermatomyositis?
cards.back: Heliotrope rash (eyelids) and Gottron’s papules/patches (knuckles).

cards.front: Which condition is characterized by a bimodal age of onset (5–15 and 45–65 years) and proximal muscle weakness?
cards.back: Dermatomyositis.

cards.front: List the clinical features used in the Alarcon-Segovia diagnostic criteria for Mixed Connective Tissue Disease (MCTD).
cards.back: Raynaud's, swollen hands, sclerodactyly, myositis, and synovitis (with high titre anti-RNP).

cards.front: What is the defining clinical feature of 'Mechanic’s hands' in inflammatory myositis?
cards.back: Cracking and fissuring of the skin of the finger pads.

cards.front: In the approach to arthritis, how is 'polyarthritis' defined by the number of joints involved?
cards.back: Involvement of 5 or more joints.

cards.front: Which pattern of arthritis is typically associated with Osteoarthritis: Symmetrical or Asymmetrical?
cards.back: Asymmetrical (though it can be generalized, it lacks the 'true' symmetry of RA).

cards.front: Which inflammatory markers are typically elevated in Rheumatoid Arthritis but normal in Osteoarthritis?
cards.back: ESR (Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate) and CRP (C-reactive protein).

cards.front: What diagnostic maneuver is used to assess lumbar spine flexion in Spondyloarthritis?
cards.back: Modified Schober test.

cards.front: What is 'Circinate balanitis'?
cards.back: Shallow, painless ulcers on the glans penis associated with Reactive Arthritis.

cards.front: In the context of back pain, what structures constitute the 'vertebral arch'?
cards.back: Pedicles, laminae, facet joints, transverse processes, and spinous processes.

cards.front: What is the significance of $C3$ and $C4$ complement levels in SLE management?
cards.back: Low levels typically indicate active disease (consumption due to immune complexes).

cards.front: Which medication is considered the 'gold standard' anchor DMARD for Rheumatoid Arthritis?
cards.back: Methotrexate.

cards.front: In Scleroderma, what does the term 'Microstomia' describe?
cards.back: Reduction in the size of the mouth opening due to skin tightening.

cards.front: Which connective tissue disease is associated with recurrent miscarriages and venous/arterial thrombosis?
cards.back: Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome (APLS).

cards.front: Identify the pattern: A 25-year-old male with acute onset of swollen knee, conjunctivitis, and recent diarrhea.
cards.back: Reactive Arthritis.

cards.front: In Gout, what are 'Interval Phase' periods?
cards.back: Asymptomatic periods between acute gouty attacks.

cards.front: What is the primary cause of joint pain in Osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Mechanical stress, inflammation of the synovium, and subchondral bone micro-fractures.

cards.front: How does 'inflammatory' spinal pain typically respond to exercise versus rest?
cards.back: It improves with exercise and worsens with rest (unlike mechanical pain).

cards.front: Which drug is used for patients with SLE specifically to prevent flares and manage skin/joint symptoms?
cards.back: Chloroquine (or Hydroxychloroquine).

cards.front: Which immunosuppressant is preferred for managing renal disease (Lupus Nephritis) in SLE?
cards.back: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) or Cyclophosphamide.

cards.front: What is the 'Shawl-sign' in Dermatomyositis?
cards.back: A rash distributed over the shoulders and proximal arms.

cards.front: What is the 'Holster-sign' in Dermatomyositis?
cards.back: A rash on the lateral aspect of the proximal thighs.

cards.front: In the EULAR criteria for RA, how many points are awarded for involvement of $> 10$ joints (with at least 1 small joint)?
cards.back: 5 points.

cards.front: What is the effect of $TNF$ and $IL-1$ on chondrocytes in the RA joint?
cards.back: They stimulate chondrocytes to destroy cartilage.

cards.front: Which class of drugs includes Chloroquine, Sulphasalazine, Methotrexate, and Leflunomide?
cards.back: Conventional Synthetic DMARDs (csDMARDs).

cards.front: What is 'Adhesive capsulitis' in the context of shoulder pain?
cards.back: A condition (Frozen Shoulder) causing pain and global limitation of shoulder movement.

cards.front: In Spondyloarthritis, what is the 'Occiput to wall' test used to measure?
cards.back: Cervical spine mobility and forward stoop (kyphosis).

cards.front: Which subtype of SpA is most likely to present with 'pitting of the nails'?
cards.back: Psoriatic Arthritis.

cards.front: What is 'secondary' Osteoarthritis?
cards.back: OA that occurs due to a preceding cause such as trauma, metabolic disease, or joint deformity.

cards.front: Which medication is used to treat Chlamydia-induced Reactive Arthritis to reduce relapses?
cards.back: Tetracyclines (e.g., Lymecycline).

cards.front: In Gout, what are the two main 'Secondary' causes of urate overproduction?
cards.back: Malignancies (high cell turnover) and haemolytic anaemia.

cards.front: Why is 'asymptomatic hyperuricaemia' generally not treated with urate-lowering drugs?
cards.back: The risk of developing gout or renal insufficiency is low for most individuals.

cards.front: Identify the ANA staining pattern most specific for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus.
cards.back: Peripheral or rim pattern.

cards.front: In Scleroderma management, which class of drugs is used to treat Raynaud's phenomenon?
cards.back: Calcium channel blockers (e.g., Nifedipine or Amlodipine).

cards.front: Which inflammatory myopathy is characterized by 'Inclusion bodies' and is often resistant to therapy?
cards.back: Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM).

cards.front: What is the typical ratio of females to males affected by Rheumatoid Arthritis?
What are the three clinical stages of Scleroderma skin involvement?
cards.back: Oedematous, Indurated, and Atrophic.

cards.front: List three risk factors for the development of 'Secondary' Raynaud's.
cards.back: Connective tissue disease, positive ANF, and abnormal nail fold capillary microscopy.

cards.front: What is the role of Occupational Therapy in RA management?
cards.back: Patient education, splinting, joint protection techniques, and preserving ADL function.

cards.front: In Gout, which drug class can precipitate an attack by causing 'under-excretion' of uric acid?
cards.back: Diuretics (e.g., Thiazides).

cards.front: Name the specific JAK kinase inhibitor mentioned for the treatment of SpA.
cards.back: Tofacitinib.

cards.front: What is 'atlanto-axial subluxation' in RA?
cards.back: Instability between the C1 and C2 vertebrae, a potentially serious cervical spine complication.

cards.front: What is the lifetime prevalence and point prevalence of neck pain?
cards.back: Neck pain has a lifetime prevalence of approximately 71%. Point prevalence affects approximately 9.5% of males and 13.5% of females.

cards.front: What occupational and personal factors are associated with neck pain?
cards.back: Neck pain is associated with heavy workload, level of education, depression, older age, and the nature of occupation (manual workers vs sedentary occupations).

cards.front: What structures can cause referred neck pain?
cards.back: Referred neck pain can originate from anterior neck structures such as the thyroid, pharynx, and larynx. It can also result from cervical structures including vertebrae, nerve compression, spinal cord, and surrounding musculature.

cards.front: What characterizes acute neck pain and what is its typical course?
cards.back: Acute neck pain has a short onset and is self-limiting, resolving within a short period of time with or without symptomatic treatment. It is often due to mechanical problems.

cards.front: What is torticollis (wry neck) and how does it present?
cards.back: Torticollis presents with severe pain and stiffness on awakening. X-rays are generally normal. It is often due to malalignment of the facet joints. Pain generally settles within a few days with analgesics, muscle relaxants, and botulinum toxin.

cards.front: What causes acute cervical disc herniation and how does it present?
cards.back: Acute cervical disc herniation is caused by sudden twisting movement or whiplash injury, often associated with motor vehicle accidents. It presents with severe pain aggravated by head movements, stiff neck with muscle spasm, and may have referred pain to shoulder or arm.

cards.front: What complications can cervical disc herniation cause?
cards.back: Cervical disc herniation may cause muscle weakness, diminished reflexes, and with posterior cord compression, compressive myelopathy can develop. X-rays are usually normal.

cards.front: How is acute cervical disc herniation managed?
cards.back: Treatment involves analgesics, immobilization, and a soft collar.

cards.front: What defines chronic neck pain and what are the main causes?
cards.back: Chronic neck pain is neck pain continuing for more than six weeks. It is generally due to musculoskeletal issues or disc degeneration. However, serious pathologies such as disc abscesses and neoplasms of the cervical spine must be excluded.

cards.front: What are the common causes of cervical disc abscesses?
cards.back: Pyogenic or tuberculosis infections are the most common causes of cervical disc abscesses.

cards.front: How does a cervical disc abscess present clinically?
cards.back: Disc abscesses present with severe cervical pain, very stiff neck, pain on all ranges of movement, and may cause cifosis (kyphosis). Often fever, systemic upset, and bone tenderness are present with elevated inflammatory markers (ESR) and raised white cell count. X-rays may show destruction of the disc and adjacent bone.

cards.front: How do cervical neoplasms present?
cards.back: Cervical neoplasms present with fever and weight loss. Pain is usually greatest at night and when lying down. May have infiltration of the spinal cord and vertebral column, metastasis from primary carcinoma, extensive root signs, and lytic lesions on x-rays.

cards.front: Are all cervical spinal tumors malignant?
cards.back: No. Benign tumors such as neurofibromas can occur.

cards.front: What age-related red flags should raise suspicion for serious neck pain?
cards.back: In older people, higher suspicion for malignancies and metastatic bone disease. In younger people, higher suspicion for inflammatory pathology such as spondyloarthropathies.

cards.front: Why is surgical history important in neck pain evaluation?
cards.back: If a patient has recently or previously had surgery, always consider infectious pathologies for neck pain, especially if it is non-mechanical in nature.

cards.front: What non-mechanical sources can cause referred neck pain?
cards.back: Pharyngitis, laryngitis, colitis, tracheitis, thyroiditis, lymphadenitis, carotid dissection, myocardial infarction, pericarditis, and diaphragmatic inflammation or infection can cause referred neck pain.

cards.front: What is polymyalgia rheumatica and who does it affect?
cards.back: Polymyalgia rheumatica is a rheumatic disease affecting women older than 60 years. Patients present with pain and stiffness in the proximal muscles of the shoulders and thighs, with immobility or morning stiffness on first rising.

cards.front: How does polymyositis present?
cards.back: Polymyositis presents with widespread myalgia, specifically involving proximal muscles of the shoulder girdle and hip girdle. Associated with abnormal lab tests or elevated creatinine kinase levels.

cards.front: How can rheumatoid arthritis affect the cervical spine?
cards.back: Rheumatoid arthritis may cause inflammatory neck pain. Associated findings include polyarthritis affecting other joints (hands, feet, knees). Flexion-extension view of C1-C2 can show subluxation of the joint if rheumatoid involvement of the cervical spine is present.

cards.front: How is ankylosing spondylitis diagnosed if cervical x-rays are normal?
cards.back: If neck x-rays do not reveal syndesmophytes, a Ferguson view of the pelvis may be useful for sacroiliitis and can help with diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis.

cards.front: What are the clinical signs and diagnostic criteria for fibromyalgia?
cards.back: Fibromyalgia is diagnosed by tender points (localized pain on digital pressure). Patients benefit from aerobic exercise and antidepressants. Classification criteria include tender points and trigger points (radiation of muscle pain distal to area of pressure).

cards.front: How is myofascial pain (trigger points) managed?
cards.back: Management involves injection of local anesthetic and corticosteroids.

cards.front: What are the mechanical effects of flexion trauma to the neck?
cards.back: Flexion trauma results in compressive forces to vertebral bodies and discs, and destructive forces on the posterior spinal elements and neck muscles.

cards.front: What are the effects of extension injuries to the neck and their prognosis?
cards.back: Extension injuries compress posterior cervical structures and distract anterior components (disc, anterior longitudinal ligament). Extension injuries carry a worse prognosis for development of chronic pain.

cards.front: How should pain quality be classified in neck pain evaluation?
cards.back: Pain should be classified as mechanical (dull, deep aching), shooting (electrical type, indicating neural involvement), or deep unremitting (due to ongoing distention, deformity, or invasion of pain-sensitive structures).

cards.front: What characteristics define mechanical pain?
cards.back: Mechanical pain is experienced in episodes, related to movement and exertion, made worse by particular postures and exercise, and improved by rest and immobility.

cards.front: What pain characteristics should alert to a sinister underlying cause?
cards.back: Pain that is not altered by movement and worsens spontaneously should alert one to a more sinister underlying cause.

cards.front: What is the significance of pain radiation in neck pain?
cards.back: Intrinsic neck pain radiates into the head and down onto the shoulders. Headache is common with upper cervical pathology (C3 and above). Pain from lower cervical areas radiates to shoulder girdle, arm, interscapular area, and chest wall.

cards.front: What cervical nerve roots cause different paresthesia patterns?
cards.back: Paresthesias that change with posture and affect head/trunk indicate C1-C3 nerve root involvement. Numbness of neck, shoulders, arm, forearm, and fingers indicate C5-C7 nerve root involvement.

cards.front: What clinical signs indicate cervical nerve root disorders?
cards.back: Signs include loss of reflexes, dermatomal sensory loss, and myotomal motor weakness. Nerve conduction studies can confirm these findings.

cards.front: What should general examination assess in neck pain?
cards.back: General examination should look for lymph nodes, examine other joints, perform abdominal and breast examination, and include neurological examination to exclude non-mechanical causes.

cards.front: What specific aspects should local examination of the neck address?
cards.back: Observe posture, neck movement, gait, spinal deformities, and muscle wasting. Palpate larynx, thyroid, lymph nodes, carotid arteries, trapezius, levator scapulae, and rhomboid muscles for tenderness.

cards.front: What movements should be assessed at the cervical spine?
cards.back: Movements include flexion, extension, left lateral rotation, right lateral rotation, left lateral flexion, and right lateral flexion.

cards.front: What do plain x-rays of the neck assess and what are the useful views?
cards.back: Plain x-rays are useful in trauma to detect fractures and subluxations. Useful views include lateral flexion, extension, and open mouth views. Also useful for excluding bone disorders (Paget disease, malignancy, osteomyelitis) and rheumatic disorders (DISH, ankylosing spondylitis).

cards.front: What is the significance of degenerative changes on cervical x-rays?
cards.back: Cervical spondylosis can be diagnosed on x-ray, but the presence of degenerative changes does not always correlate with neck pain symptoms.

cards.front: When is CT vs MRI preferred for neck pain evaluation?
cards.back: CT is valuable for assessing bone structures, especially foraminal stenosis. MRI is sensitive for disc herniation, intercord pathology, cord compression, and bone infection or inflammation (osteomyelitis). However, 20% of asymptomatic patients and 60% of older patients have abnormal MRI findings.

cards.front: What is the role of bone scan in neck pain evaluation?
cards.back: Bone scan (isotope scan) is sensitive for bony pathology (metastasis, infection) especially where plain x-rays are normal. It may assist in differentiating inflammatory versus degenerative changes.

cards.front: What laboratory investigations are useful in neck pain?
cards.back: No specific role for laboratory investigations in mechanical neck pain. However, if suspicious of tumors or infection, full blood count (elevated white cell count), elevated ESR, CRP, and raised alkaline phosphatase are useful.

cards.front: What are first-line analgesics for neck pain?
cards.back: Simple analgesics such as paracetamol are safe over a long period of time.

cards.front: When are opioids indicated for neck pain and what are the cautions?
cards.back: Opioids such as propoxyphene and codeine are for severe refractory cases. However, they should be used with caution as they have high propensity for abuse and dependency.

cards.front: Why are antidepressants used in chronic neck pain?
cards.back: Tricyclic antidepressants such as amitriptyline are used at low doses for nocturnal sedation. Antidepressants are needed because chronic pain is often associated with depression.

cards.front: What are the goals of exercise in neck pain management?
cards.back: The two principal aims are to restore normal range of movement and strengthen neck musculature. Exercise helps prevent acute exacerbations.

cards.front: What physical therapy modalities are used for neck pain?
cards.back: Physical therapy includes heat or cold therapy, ultrasound, traction, and massage. These may have some limited benefit.

cards.front: What is the role of manipulation in neck pain?
cards.back: Manipulative or chiropractic manipulation aims to correct vertebral subluxation, stretch tight joint capsules, and mobilization through oscillatory movements may have small benefit.

cards.front: What ergonomic interventions are helpful in neck pain?
cards.back: Interventions include posture correction, modification of workplace environment, ergonomic adjustments, and modification of seating devices to decrease extreme neck movements and time in fixed cervical posture.

cards.front: What is the evidence for zygapophysial joint injections in neck pain?
cards.back: Injection of the zygapophysial joint with local anesthetic or steroid is generally of no benefit.

cards.front: What is the natural history of cervical nerve root compression?
cards.back: The majority of cases resolve in a few weeks to months. However, less than 10% have persisting moderate or severe disability.

cards.front: Is there a difference between cervical collar, physiotherapy, and surgery for nerve compression?
cards.back: No long-term difference exists between cervical collar, physiotherapy, or surgery. The choice hinges upon minimizing harm.

cards.front: What role do traction and TENS play in cervical nerve compression?
cards.back: Cervical traction and transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) may be used as pain treatment modalities. Isometric exercises may also be beneficial.

cards.front: Does surgery improve long-term outcomes in cervical nerve compression?
cards.back: Surgery improves symptoms faster than conservative measures but has no long-term difference when compared to conservative treatment.

cards.front: What is the definition of low back pain?
cards.back: Low back pain is pain localized below the line of the 12th rib and above the inferior gluteal folds, with or without leg pain.

cards.front: How is low back pain classified?
cards.back: Low back pain may be specific (identifiable pathological cause) or non-specific (no known disease or pathogenic mechanism; anatomical source obscure).

cards.front: What is the prevalence of low back pain?
cards.back: Lifetime prevalence is 75-85%. Yearly prevalence in USA is 15-20% and in Europe is 25-45%. In UK, 5.5-7.5% of GP consultations are for low back pain.

cards.front: What is the natural history of work absence due to back pain?
cards.back: Only 50% of patients off work for more than six months return to work. Almost nobody returns to work after two years of absence.

cards.front: What are the occupational risk factors for back pain?
cards.back: Increased association with heavy physical work, static work posture, and psychosocial factors. Highest risk in nurses and drivers.

cards.front: What is the recurrence rate of back pain?
cards.back: Lifetime recurrence is up to 85%. In Sweden, one-year recurrence was 44%. In Canada, one-year recurrence was 20% and three-year recurrence was 36%.

cards.front: What is the impact of back pain on work loss?
cards.back: Back pain is a major cause of sick leave. Average sick days in 1983-84 were 28.9 days, increasing to 105.8 days in 1993-94.

cards.front: What is the relationship between chronic back pain and psychiatric symptoms?
cards.back: Chronic back pain is often associated with psychiatric symptoms (depression, substance abuse, anxiety, personality disorders) that often precede the onset of back pain.

cards.front: What are key epidemiological facts about back pain?
cards.back: Back pain affects two-thirds of adults. It is second only to upper respiratory tract infection in frequency. Affects men and women equally. Most common between 30-50 years. It is an expensive cause of work-related disability.

cards.front: What percentage of back pain is mechanical and what are the causes?
cards.back: 90% of low back pain is mechanical and due to muscular ligamentous injuries, age-related degeneration of intervertebral discs and facet joints, spinal stenosis, disc herniation, osteoporotic compression fractures, spondylosis, and spondylolysis.

cards.front: What is the natural history and spontaneous recovery rate of back pain?
cards.back: 50% are better after one week. More than 90% are better at 8 weeks. Only 7-10% of back pain persists beyond 6 months.

cards.front: What are the medical causes of low back pain?
cards.back: Medical causes include spondyloarthropathies, spinal infections, osteoporosis, malignancy, and referred pain from pelvic structures, renal structures, aortic aneurysm, and pancreatitis.

cards.front: What are the key clinical questions in back pain evaluation?
cards.back: Is systemic disease causing the pain? Is there neurological compromise requiring surgical evaluation? Is there social or psychological distress amplifying or prolonging pain?

cards.front: What is the general approach to back pain management?
cards.back: Management involves watchful waiting. Patient education is important. Spontaneous recovery is the rule. Those who remain active despite pain have less future chronic pain. Patients should rest for 2 days or less. Reassess if pain worsens or neurological symptoms develop.

cards.front: What is the role of exercise in back pain?
cards.back: Exercise has prevention power and helps prevent recurrences and treat chronic pain. However, exercise is not useful for the acute phase.

cards.front: Why should imaging studies be used cautiously in back pain?
cards.back: Imaging studies can be misleading. Many abnormalities are common in pain-free individuals. In people under 60, there is low yield for unexpected x-ray findings.

cards.front: How common are abnormal disc findings in asymptomatic individuals?
cards.back: In asymptomatic individuals under 60: spondylosis in 1 of 2,500, bulging discs in 1 of 3 patients, herniated discs in 1 of 5 patients. Over 60: herniated discs in 1 of 3, bulging discs in 80%, disc and apophysial joint degeneration in all, spinal stenosis in 1 of 5 cases.

cards.front: What are the red flags for serious back pain?
cards.back: Red flags include fever, weight loss, pain worse when lying down and worse at night, morning stiffness, pain lasting more than 6 weeks, age over 50 with new onset pain, abnormal neurological signs, and point tenderness.

cards.front: What is the goal of further evaluation in back pain and what studies are useful?
cards.back: Goal is to discriminate between benign causes and disorders requiring diagnostic studies. Diagnostic studies include x-rays, CT scans, and MRI. Useful lab tests include full blood count, ESR, calcium, alkaline phosphatase, and protein electrophoresis.

cards.front: What serious conditions should be considered in back pain?
cards.back: Herniated disc, spinal stenosis, cauda equina syndrome, inflammatory spondyloarthropathy, spinal infection, vertebral fracture, malignancy, and referred visceral pain.

cards.front: What is spinal stenosis?
cards.back: Spinal stenosis shows on imaging (CT/MRI) as canal narrowing. CT myelogram shows canal occlusion with flexion due to spondylolisthesis.

cards.front: How does disc herniation appear on imaging?
cards.back: CT imaging shows a protruding disc compressing the thecal sac.

cards.front: What are the imaging features of ankylosing spondylitis?
cards.back: Features include sacroiliitis (inflammation of sacroiliac joints) and vertebral syndesmophytes (bony bridging), which create a bamboo spine appearance.

cards.front: How does spinal infection appear on imaging?
cards.back: Imaging shows disc involvement with spinal infection and pelvic bone involvement in osteomyelitis.

cards.front: How do osteoporotic compression fractures appear on x-ray?
cards.back: X-rays show multiple wedge fractures with markedly reduced vertebral body density.

cards.front: What are the red flags for spinal malignancy?
cards.back: Pain is worse at night, often associated with localized bone thinness. Blood count, ESR, and protein electrophoresis would be abnormal with elevated ESR. Abnormal x-ray findings should raise suspicion.

cards.front: When is surgical referral indicated for sciatica?
cards.back: Surgical referral is indicated for sciatica with probable herniated disc, progressive or severe neurological deficit, or persistent sciatica with consistent neurologic and clinical findings after 4-6 weeks of conservative treatment.

cards.front: When is surgical referral indicated for spinal stenosis?
cards.back: Surgical referral is indicated if there is progressive or severe neurological deficit, persistent back and leg pain that improves with flexion and is associated with spinal stenosis on imaging.

cards.front: When is surgical referral indicated for spondylolisthesis?
cards.back: Surgical referral is indicated if there is progressive or severe spondylolisthesis with severe neurological deficit, severe back pain and sciatica with functional impairment persisting more than one year.

cards.front: What are the indicators for surgical intervention in back pain?
cards.back: Indicators include debility (inability to perform activities of daily living and functional impairment) and unbearable pain.

cards.front: What are the key clinical points about low back pain?
cards.back: 90% are due to mechanical causes and resolve spontaneously within 6 weeks to 6 months. Diagnostic workup is needed for red flags. ESR elevation warrants evaluation for malignancy or infection. Initial x-rays are beneficial in older patients. Rest is not recommended. Return to normal activities is most beneficial. Back exercises prevent recurrences and treat chronic pain. Surgery is appropriate for small proportion of patients.

cards.front: What is cauda equina syndrome and when is it a surgical emergency?
cards.back: Cauda equina syndrome is a serious neurological emergency requiring urgent surgical evaluation and intervention when there is progressive or severe neurological deficit.

cards.front: What is the primary characteristic of osteoarthritis pathology?
cards.back: Focal softening and disintegration of articular cartilage accompanied by proliferation of new bone (osteophyte formation on joint margins).

cards.front: What are the three main x-ray findings in osteoarthritis?
cards.back: 1) Joint space narrowing, 2) Subchondral sclerosis, 3) Marginal osteophyte formation.

cards.front: What is the role of the collagen network in cartilage?
cards.back: The collagen network maintains cartilage strength and integrity while preserving water content and hydration of the cartilage.

cards.front: What happens when the collagen network is disrupted?
cards.back: Cartilage integrity is compromised, leading to erosion, damage, dehydration, and microfractures in the subchondral bone.

cards.front: What is the blood supply source for cartilage nutrients?
cards.back: Cartilage is avascular (non-vascular) and receives nutrients from the vascular supply of the subchondral bone.

cards.front: What stimuli trigger the pathogenic cascade in osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Trauma, mechanical injury, or metabolic factors that stress the cartilage and affect chondrocytes.

cards.front: What do damaged chondrocytes produce?
cards.back: Enzymes that break down cartilage and collagen network, plus alkaline phosphatases that further damage cartilage integrity.

cards.front: What is the primary function of cartilage in joints?
cards.back: To act as a shock absorber, with synovial fluid lining the joint to prevent excessive stress and redistribute forces.

cards.front: What structures are involved in osteoarthritic joint damage?
cards.back: Joint capsule (thickening), cartilage (fibrillation), synovium (hypertrophy), and subchondral bone (sclerosis, cyst formation).

cards.front: What causes synovial hypertrophy in osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Inflammatory infiltrate within the synovium and fluid buildup, leading to stretching of the joint capsule.

cards.front: What is the prevalence of symptomatic knee osteoarthritis in UK population over 55?
cards.back: 9.5% of population aged over 55 have symptomatic knee osteoarthritis (Framingham study).

cards.front: What is radiographic osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Incidental x-ray findings of osteoarthritis in asymptomatic patients showing features like joint space narrowing and osteophytes.

cards.front: What percentage of adults in the US have radiographic hand OA?
cards.back: 32.5% of adults in the US have radiographic hand osteoarthritis.

cards.front: Who is more commonly affected by osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Women more than men, particularly postmenopausal women; peak onset between ages 50-60 years.

cards.front: What is the difference between primary and secondary osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Primary OA has idiopathic cause; secondary OA has a known preceding cause (trauma, surgery, inflammatory conditions, etc.).

cards.front: What is the distinction between localized and generalized osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Localized OA involves less than 3 joint areas; generalized OA involves 3 or more joint areas.

cards.front: What is nodal osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Bony swelling of small hand joints, particularly proximal and distal interphalangeal joints and first carpometacarpal joint, associated with collagen 2A gene abnormalities.

cards.front: What is the typical duration of morning stiffness in osteoarthritis?
cards.back: Usually less than 30 minutes, occurring after inactivity (unlike inflammatory arthritis which lasts >30 minutes to 1 hour).

cards.front: How does pain in osteoarthritis differ from inflammatory arthritis pain?
